patterns.what_to_look_for: Password-reset tokens that are predictable, reusable, unbound to the requesting user, never expired, or not invalidated after a successful reset. Watch for tokens derived from Math.random()/timestamps/sequential IDs, tokens echoed back in the response or URL and accepted again, and reset endpoints that take a user_id/email in the body alongside the token (letting one user's valid token reset another account).
source_indicators: Token generated via Math.random(), Date.now(), uuid v1, or md5(email+timestamp) instead of crypto.randomBytes/secrets.token_urlsafe
Reset record lacks expires_at / used_at columns or they are never checked
resetPassword(token, userId, newPassword) takes a separate user id/email not derived from the token row
No DELETE/UPDATE marking the token consumed after password change
Token compared with == / string equality instead of constant-time compare
blackbox_indicators: Reset link token is short/sequential/base64 of an integer and increments across requests
Re-submitting the same reset link after use still returns 200 and changes the password
POST /reset accepts email/user_id field that can be swapped to a victim while keeping attacker's token
Reset token still valid hours/days later (no expiry)
Host header or X-Forwarded-Host reflected into the emailed reset link (poisoning)
evidence_required: Captured reset request/response showing token structure or reflected identifier
Replay of a used token producing a second successful reset (HTTP 200 + login with new password)
Demonstration that a token bound to account A resets account B, or that expiry is absent
false_positive_checks: Confirm token is actually high-entropy crypto-random before claiming predictability
Verify the user_id in the body is server-validated against the token's owner, not trusted
Check whether a short TTL or single-use invalidation exists server-side even if not obvious in one file
Rule out test/staging seed accounts that legitimately allow repeated resets
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Request two resets for an attacker-owned account and check token entropy/sequence, then replay the first (already-used) token once to see if it is still accepted.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as account takeover via password reset; cite token entropy/binding/expiry/single-use defect and the exact replay that succeeded.

patterns.what_to_look_for: MFA that is enforced only in the UI or on the wrong endpoint, leaving a fully authenticated session before the second factor is verified. Look for login that issues the real session cookie/JWT first and only then prompts for OTP, an /mfa/verify step that can be skipped by calling protected APIs directly, and OTP verification with no attempt limit / no lockout (brute-forceable 6-digit code).
source_indicators: Session/JWT issued in the password step, with a separate mfa_passed flag that protected routes never check
OTP verify endpoint has no rate limit, attempt counter, or code-expiry; code stored in plaintext/session
Client-side gating only (if (user.mfaRequired) showOtpScreen) with no server middleware
Backup-code or 'remember this device' path that bypasses verify and sets mfa_passed=true
OTP compared with == and not invalidated after success (reusable)
blackbox_indicators: After password step the session cookie already grants access to /api/* without completing OTP
Skipping the OTP screen and directly requesting a post-login page returns authorized content
OTP endpoint accepts unlimited guesses (no 429 / lockout) for a 6-digit code
A second login endpoint (mobile/legacy/API) returns a token without prompting for MFA
Same OTP code works more than once or after its displayed validity window
evidence_required: Request sequence showing an authorized protected response while MFA state is incomplete
Captured rate-limit absence (e.g. 100 OTP attempts all 200/incorrect, no 429)
Identification of the alternate endpoint or flag that skips server-side MFA enforcement
false_positive_checks: Confirm the pre-MFA token is not a restricted 'mfa-pending' scope that protected routes reject
Verify protected endpoints truly accept the session, not just return a generic page
Check whether a WAF/edge rate-limit catches OTP brute force even if app code does not
Ensure the 'alternate endpoint' isn't itself behind its own MFA challenge
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Complete only the password step, then issue one benign authenticated request (e.g. GET /api/me) to check whether protected data is returned before OTP is verified.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as MFA bypass / second-factor not enforced server-side; show the exact request that succeeded without a valid second factor.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Session identifier not rotated on privilege transition - i.e. the same session ID/cookie survives across the anonymous->authenticated boundary (session fixation) and across role/privilege escalation. Look for login handlers that set user data into an existing session without regenerating the ID, frameworks where session.regenerate()/session_regenerate_id() is missing, and session IDs accepted from a URL parameter.
source_indicators: Login sets req.session.userId without req.session.regenerate() (Express), session_regenerate_id() missing (PHP), or Django login() not called
Session ID read from query string / URL (?sid=, ?PHPSESSID=) or accepted from a request param
Cookie set without HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite, enabling a pre-set fixed value to persist
Privilege/role elevation updates session in place with no ID rotation
Custom session store keyed on a client-supplied token reused before and after auth
blackbox_indicators: Session cookie value is identical before login and after a successful login
A session ID injected before authentication (attacker-chosen) remains valid and authenticated post-login
Server honors a session ID supplied via URL parameter
No new Set-Cookie issued on the login response
Cookie lacks HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite in Set-Cookie
evidence_required: Pre-auth and post-auth cookie capture showing the same session value
Demonstration that an attacker-fixed session ID becomes authenticated after the victim logs in
Login response headers showing no session rotation (Set-Cookie absent or unchanged)
false_positive_checks: Confirm the unchanged cookie is the actual session ID, not a static CSRF/anti-CSRF or analytics cookie
Verify the framework isn't rotating server-side session data while keeping a stable opaque cookie name
Check that SameSite/Secure flags don't already neutralize the fixation vector in the target context
Rule out load-balancer affinity cookies being mistaken for the session token
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Capture the session cookie as an anonymous user, log in with a test account, and compare the cookie value before vs after to confirm whether the ID rotates.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as session fixation; cite the unchanged session ID across the auth boundary and the path by which an attacker can pre-seed it.

patterns.what_to_look_for: JWTs accepted without proper signature/claims validation: 'alg':'none' accepted, algorithm confusion (RS256 token re-signed as HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret), decode-without-verify, missing exp/nbf checks, or unvalidated aud/iss. Look for jwt.decode() used instead of jwt.verify(), verify called with algorithms allowing 'none' or both RS256+HS256, and trusting client claims like role/is_admin in the payload.
source_indicators: jwt.decode(token) / jwt.sign without verify, or verify({ algorithms: ['none', ...] })
verify accepts both ['RS256','HS256'] (alg-confusion exposure) or reads alg from the token header
No exp/nbf validation, or ignoreExpiration:true
Secret is weak/hardcoded ('secret', 'changeme') for HS256
Authorization decisions read role/admin/scope straight from decoded payload with no server-side re-check
blackbox_indicators: Token with header {"alg":"none"} and stripped signature is accepted
Expired token (exp in the past) still authorizes requests
Tampering a base64 payload claim (e.g. role=admin) without re-signing is accepted
Token signed with HS256 using the published RS256 public key is accepted
Same JWT works against a different audience/tenant than it was issued for
evidence_required: A forged/altered token (alg:none, modified claim, or HS256-confusion) that yields an authorized response
An expired token producing a 200 on a protected endpoint
Source location showing decode-without-verify or permissive algorithm list
false_positive_checks: Confirm the endpoint actually trusts the JWT (not a separate server-side session) before claiming bypass
Verify the library version doesn't already reject 'none'/alg-confusion by default
Check that a gateway/edge validates the token even if the app code looks permissive
Ensure the 'accepted' forged token isn't just returning a generic/unauthenticated page
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Take a valid token for an attacker account, set its exp to the past (or header alg to none with empty signature), and send one request to a protected read endpoint to see if it is still authorized.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as JWT signature/claims validation bypass; name the exact defect (none, alg-confusion, expiry, aud/iss, trusted client claim) and show the forged token that was accepted.

patterns.what_to_look_for: OAuth/OIDC callback handlers missing CSRF state validation, with open-redirect-influenced redirect_uri, or weak account linking that trusts an unverified email claim. Look for /callback that reads the code and exchanges it without comparing the returned state to the session-stored value, redirect_uri/return_to taken from a request param and not allow-listed, and account linking/login that keys on email from the IdP without checking email_verified.
source_indicators: Callback handler exchanges 'code' but never compares 'state' to a server-stored nonce
redirect_uri / return_to / next read from query param and passed through without an allow-list
Account lookup/link by IdP email without checking email_verified == true
State generated but stored client-side only (cookie the attacker can set) instead of bound to session
PKCE code_verifier not used for public clients / not validated
blackbox_indicators: Callback accepts a request with a missing or attacker-chosen state and still logs in
redirect_uri can be pointed at an attacker domain or an open-redirect on the trusted host to leak the code
Logging in via an IdP with an unverified attacker-set email lands on a pre-existing victim account
No state parameter present in the initial authorization redirect
Reusing a previously seen authorization code is accepted
evidence_required: Callback request with forged/absent state completing authentication
Demonstration that redirect_uri/return_to can be redirected off-origin to capture the code/token
Account-linking flow where an unverified email claim attaches to or logs into a different user
false_positive_checks: Confirm state is genuinely unvalidated server-side, not validated in middleware before the handler
Verify redirect_uri is not actually checked against the IdP's registered allow-list (IdP-side enforcement)
Check whether email_verified is enforced elsewhere in the linking path
Rule out PKCE/nonce binding that already prevents the forged-state attack
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Initiate the OAuth login, then replay the callback once with the state parameter removed (or altered) to observe whether authentication still completes.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as OAuth login CSRF / account takeover via callback; specify the missing control (state binding, redirect_uri allow-list, or email_verified) and show the request that bypassed it.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Sessions/tokens that remain valid after logout, password change, or role downgrade - i.e. logout only clears the client cookie, and privileges are read from a long-lived token never re-checked. Look for logout handlers that just res.clearCookie() / delete client storage without destroying the server session or revoking the token, stateless JWTs with long TTL and no deny-list, and authorization that trusts roles baked into an old token after the role was revoked.
source_indicators: logout() only clears the cookie / localStorage and never calls session.destroy() or token revocation
Stateless JWT with long exp and no revocation/deny-list/jti tracking
Password-change / role-change handlers don't bump a token version, sessions_valid_after, or invalidate existing sessions
Authorization reads role/permissions from the token payload, not from current DB state
Refresh tokens not rotated or revoked on logout/password change
blackbox_indicators: A captured session cookie/JWT still authorizes protected requests after the user clicks logout
Old session still works after the account's password is changed
A token issued while the user was admin still grants admin after the role is revoked (until exp)
Refresh token continues minting access tokens after logout
Concurrent sessions are not terminated by 'log out everywhere' / password reset
evidence_required: Replay of the pre-logout token producing an authorized 200 after logout
Replay of a session after a password change still succeeding
Demonstration that a downgraded user's old token retains elevated access
false_positive_checks: Confirm the replayed token is the real session credential, not a cached static asset response
Verify there is no short TTL + immediate revocation that closes the window in practice
Check whether a server-side deny-list/token-version invalidates the token despite client-only logout code
Ensure observed 'access' is genuinely authorized data, not a public/unauthenticated endpoint
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Authenticate a test account, save the session token, log out (or change its password) in another request, then replay the saved token once against GET /api/me to see if it is still authorized.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as session/token not invalidated on logout/credential or role change; cite the exact post-event replay that remained authorized and the missing server-side revocation.
